--- SOURCE 1: 5G Connectivity and Drones | ---
URL: https://www.thedroningcompany.com/blog/5g-connectivity-and-drones

SUMMARY:
Enhanced Data Transmission: 5G facilitates rapid data transfer, enabling drones to transmit large volumes of data in real-time. High-definition

FULL CONTENT:
![](https://www.facebook.com/tr?id=744131293231822&ev=PageView&noscript=1)
![](https://www.facebook.com/tr?id=327731249272380&ev=PageView&noscript=1)

## [Featured News](#)[Trending News](#)[Akshata](#)[5G Connectivity and Drones](/blog/5g-connectivity-and-drones "5G Connectivity and Drones")

![post1](https://media.thedroningcompany.com/images/blog/1712557013.png)

*08 April 2024*

**By Akshata**

5G connectivity refers to the fifth generation of mobile networks, characterized by significantly higher speeds, reduced latency, and increased capacity compared to its predecessors. When applied to drones, 5G connectivity becomes a game changer by providing a robust and reliable communication infrastructure. The integration of 5G connectivity with drones is opening up possibilities and this synergy is not only transforming the way drones operate but also enhancing their potential applications across various sectors. Utilizing 5G connectivity, drones can establish faster and more stable communication channels with ground control and other devices. This enables real-time data transmission, high-definition video streaming, and precise control, making 5G-equipped drones exceptionally versatile in various applications.

##### **Benefits of 5G Connectivity for Drones**

The integration of 5G connectivity into drones is already in progress, with major industry players actively pursuing and implementing this advanced technology. Below are 5 benefits of using 5G connectivity:

##### **5G-Enabled Drone Solutions**

**Nokia's FCC-Certified Drone Networks Solution.**In collaboration with Rohde & Schwarz, **[Nokia](https://www.fiercewireless.com/tech/nokia-brings-4g5g-drone-box-solution-us)** achieved a significant milestone as its Drone Networks solution received full certification from the Federal Communications Commission (FCC). This marks a historic moment as the first-ever certification for a native 4G/5G drone-in-a-box solution. Tailored to serve as a shareable data collection platform, the solution is positioned to elevate public safety and industrial applications. Industrial users can seamlessly book drone flights, leveraging native 4G/5G connectivity for improved operational efficiency.

Nokia's drone solution goes beyond conventional communication protocols. Unlike drones and systems relying on Wi-Fi or additional modules for cellular connectivity, the Nokia drone with native 4G/5G connectivity leverages the broader coverage and higher data throughput of cellular networks for control and data transmission. This native integration comes "out of the box," providing an integrated connectivity solution by default.

**T-Mobile's 80-Mile Beyond-Visual-Line-of-Sight Flight.** **[T-Mobile US](https://www.mobileworldlive.com/featured-content/top-three/t-mobile-us-sets-sights-on-5g-drones/)**, in collaboration with Valmont Industries, showcased the transformative potential of 5G-enabled drones by enabling an 80-mile beyond-visual-line-of-sight flight. This groundbreaking achievement represents an industry first, highlighting 5G as a transformative force in drone capabilities. The trial, conducted with a drone supplied by Harris Aerial, demonstrated the efficiency of high-resolution video recording and near real-time analysis enabled by 5G.

The trial emphasized the utility of 5G connectivity for diverse applications, including inspection programs, agriculture, and disaster relief. T-Mobile's strategic collaboration with Valmont Industries showcased the immediate and tangible impact of 5G on extending the operational capabilities of drones. The trial's success, conducted over a distance of 77 miles, underscored the efficiency of 5G-enabled drones in visual inspections of vital infrastructure.

##### **5G Unfolds**

As the convergence of 5G connectivity and drone technology continues to unfold, the industry is witnessing groundbreaking advancements that promise to reshape industries and redefine the capabilities of unmanned aerial vehicles. The journey towards a future where 5G-enabled drones play a pivotal role in various sectors is well underway, marking a new era of connectivity, efficiency, and innovation.

# The Impact of 5G on Drone Connectivity and Performance

Feb 25, 2025

![](//insidefpv.com/cdn/shop/articles/Blog_banners_11.png?v=1750064598&width=2000)

Five years ago, drones were still kind of a gimmick. They were great for capturing vacation videos or zipping around in parks.

But today in 2025, drones have leveled up, and a big part of that leap is thanks to 5G connectivity.

Faster than Wi-Fi. Smarter than LTE. And powerful enough to run autonomous operations in real time. From [agriculture](https://insidefpv.com/pages/krishi) to [defense](https://insidefpv.com/pages/defence-drones), 5G drones are changing how industries operate, how cities are managed, and how emergencies are handled.

Let’s break down exactly how 5G has transformed drone technology, what it means for different sectors, and what’s around the corner with 6G on the horizon.

## Why 5G is an Important Development for Drones

![Drone flying with 5G connectivity for real-time data transmission and remote control](https://cdn.shopify.com/s/files/1/0556/6526/2663/files/5g-drone-technology-real-time-connectivity.png?v=1750064305)

Let us understand why 5G can be a game changer for UAVs.

#### Instant Response with Ultra-Low Latency

Traditional drone commands sometimes lagged, especially in critical moments. But 5G brings latency down to just 1 millisecond.

That means when an operator or AI system tells a drone to turn, dive, or hover, it happens instantly.

In real-world scenarios like search and rescue, disaster monitoring, or military missions, every second matters. With 5G, drones react as fast as a trained human could, only safer and with more precision.

#### Smooth Streaming of High-Res Data

5G isn’t just about faster flight, it’s also about smarter decision-making. With real-time 4K video transmission, thermal imaging, and sensor feedback, operators on the ground get crystal-clear visuals and instant data.

This is a huge deal for:

Inspecting bridges, wind turbines, and pipelines

Monitoring crop health in smart agriculture

Covering live events and news

Managing urban traffic from above

And it’s not just visual data, drones can upload and analyze cloud data while still in flight.

#### Multiple Drones, One Mission

One of the coolest 5G perks? You can now run multiple drones at once without signal crashes. These drone “swarms” are especially useful for:

Surveillance over wide zones

Coordinated agricultural spraying

Multi-angle film shoots

Emergency coverage over large disaster areas

Each drone stays connected, streams its own feed, and communicates in real-time. Think of it like Wi-Fi mesh networking, but in the sky.

#### Going Beyond the Horizon (BVLOS)

Before 5G, most drones had to stay within sight. Now, drones can fly miles away from the operator, Beyond Visual Line of Sight (BVLOS), while still sending perfect data back.

This opens up next-level use cases:

Delivery drones in remote or rural areas

Border and coast patrol operations

Surveillance of long railway lines or forests

With high-speed 5G and satellite backup, BVLOS operations are finally safe, legal, and practical.

Also read: [How drones are giving the gift of sight to India](https://insidefpv.com/blogs/blogs/how-drones-are-giving-the-gift-of-sight-in-india)

### Where Are We Seeing 5G Drones in Action?

Let us go through some of the real life use cases of 5G drones.

#### Smart Farming

Drones now scan soil, identify pests, and track water levels across acres of farmland. With 5G, this data syncs to a dashboard instantly, letting farmers take real-time action and reduce waste.

#### Emergency Response

Whether it’s forest fires, floods, or earthquakes, G-enabled drones are now the eyes in the sky. They provide live updates to first responders, helping them find survivors and plan rescue routes.

#### Delivery & Logistics

Companies are piloting (literally) 5G-powered drone deliveries in both urban and remote locations. From groceries to medicine, these deliveries are fast, trackable, and less affected by traffic or terrain.

#### Industrial Inspections

In oil rigs, construction sites, and factories, drones with 5G are replacing risky human inspections. They capture real-time video, scan for defects, and even relay thermal imaging, all from hundreds of feet up.

### What Is The Role of AI in 5G Drones?

![AI-powered drone using 5G technology flying toward a futuristic skyline](https://cdn.shopify.com/s/files/1/0556/6526/2663/files/ai-powered-5g-drones-future-horizons.png?v=1750064305)

As drones get faster and more connected with 5G, there's another powerful force quietly reshaping how they operate, artificial intelligence.

#### Edge AI

Drones are now equipped with onboard AI chips that allow real-time decision-making, no cloud connection needed. 5G acts as the link between onboard edge processing and cloud dashboards for oversight.

#### Data Transparency & Ethics

With stricter privacy rules in place, drone makers are being pushed to clearly communicate how data is collected, stored, and used. 5G enables encrypted, traceable data flows, making drone operations more accountable and trustworthy.

#### Mobile-First Command Centers

Thanks to improvements in mobile UX/UI, many drone platforms now allow full flight control via smartphones or tablets. This is done without compromising safety or precision.

Also read: [How drone-as-a-service (DaaS) model is growing globally](https://insidefpv.com/blogs/blogs/how-the-drone-as-a-service-daas-model-is-growing-globally)

### What About 6G? Here’s What’s Coming

While 5G is still maturing, work on 6G drones has already begun.

Near-Zero Latency + Ultra-HD Imaging

6G could bring latency below 0.1ms, making drone reactions faster than ever. Pair that with 8K real-time streaming and drones could become airborne AR/VR cameras.

#### Satellite-Integrated Connectivity

One of the most exciting things about 6G is that it will combine terrestrial and satellite networks. That means drones will stay connected, even in jungles, oceans, or warzones.

#### Hyper-Autonomous Missions

With 6G and AI, drones will be capable of fully autonomous missions, scanning disaster zones, delivering medical aid, or managing traffic with zero operator input.

However, there are still some challenges to solve such as:

Coverage Gaps: Rural and mountainous regions still don’t have stable 5G.

Security Threats: Better connectivity can also be exploited. Encryption and airspace management are critical.

Hardware Costs: Not every drone is 5G-ready, and upgrades can be expensive.

But as infrastructure grows and hardware becomes more affordable, these hurdles are already being tackled by industry leaders.

The evolution of drone technology, fueled by 5G, is already here. and it’s making a measurable difference in how we grow food and even deliver everyday products.

And with 6G on the way, expect drones to become even more independent, efficient, and essential.

So whether you’re a farmer, first responder, logistics expert, or simply a drone hobbyist, the next time you see a drone flying overhead, remember: it’s not just a flying camera. It’s a connected, intelligent, sky-bound machine, powered by the next generation of wireless innovation.

#### Frequently Asked Questions

###### Do all drones support 5G?

No, only certain high-end or enterprise drones currently support 5G. But support is growing fast.

###### Is 5G required for BVLOS drone flights?

While not mandatory, 5G dramatically improves safety, control, and visibility for long-range (BVLOS) operations.

###### What’s the biggest difference 6G will bring?

6G will allow global, satellite-integrated connectivity and real-time AI, enabling truly autonomous drone missions anywhere on Earth.

--- SOURCE 5: A Prototyping Platform for Evaluating Aerial 5G Communications ---
URL: https://arxiv.org/html/2506.08386

SUMMARY:
5G networks support Ultra Reliable and Low Latency Communications (URLLC) and Enhanced Mobile Broadband (eMBB), enabling UAV operations that demand ultra-reliable communications and high data rates for real-time monitoring, asset tracking, disaster recovery, and autonomy .

FULL CONTENT:
# 5G Aero: A Prototyping Platform for Evaluating Aerial 5G Communications

###### Abstract

The application of small-factor, 5G-enabled Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) has recently gained significant interest in various aerial and Industry 4.0 applications. However, ensuring reliable, high-throughput, and low-latency 5G communication in aerial applications remains a critical and underexplored problem. This paper presents the 5th generation (5G) Aero, a compact UAV optimized for 5G connectivity, aimed at fulfilling stringent 3rd Generation Partnership Project (3GPP) requirements. We conduct a set of experiments in an indoor environment, evaluating the UAV’s ability to establish high-throughput, low-latency communications in both Line-of-Sight (LoS) and Non-Line-of-Sight (NLoS) conditions. Our findings demonstrate that the 5G Aero meets the required 3GPP standards for Command and Control (C2) packets latency in both LoS and NLoS, and video latency in LoS communications and it maintains acceptable latency levels for video transmission in NLoS conditions. Additionally, we show that the 5G module installed on the UAV introduces a negligible 1% decrease in flight time, showing that 5G technologies can be integrated into commercial off-the-shelf UAVs with minimal impact on battery lifetime. This paper contributes to the literature by demonstrating the practical capabilities of current 5G networks to support advanced UAV operations in telecommunications, offering insights into potential enhancements and optimizations for UAV performance in 5G networks.

###### Index Terms:

## I Introduction

Over the past decade, Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) have evolved from niche technologies into indispensable tools across various industries. They are utilized in agriculture, logistics, and other sectors to collect data, transport goods, and handle tasks that are hazardous or time-consuming for humans [[1](https://arxiv.org/html/2506.08386v1#bib.bib1), [2](https://arxiv.org/html/2506.08386v1#bib.bib2)]. However, one of the most promising applications of UAVs is tied to the telecommunications field, where drones can serve as mobile base stations, signal relays, or aerial data collection platforms [[3](https://arxiv.org/html/2506.08386v1#bib.bib3), [4](https://arxiv.org/html/2506.08386v1#bib.bib4)].

With the advent of 5th generation (5G) technology, the role of UAVs in telecommunications is more significant than ever.
5G networks support Ultra Reliable and Low Latency Communications (URLLC) and Enhanced Mobile Broadband (eMBB), enabling UAV operations that demand ultra-reliable communications and high data rates for real-time monitoring, asset tracking, disaster recovery, and autonomy. Additionally, 5G facilitates multi-user communication over large areas, unlocking new opportunities for drone swarms and UAV applications in complex indoor Industry 4.0 environments [[5](https://arxiv.org/html/2506.08386v1#bib.bib5)].

Motivation: Despite significant advancements in UAV technologies, there remains a substantial gap in the development of compact and agile UAV platforms with 5G capabilities for telecommunication research. Not only does the consumer drone market offer relatively few options, but it is also often expensive and lacks the necessary open Application Programming Interfaces (APIs) and configurable 5G modules. Furthermore, existing UAVs designed for 5G research tend to be large or prohibitively costly, making them unsuitable for constrained environments such as indoor spaces and narrow outdoor areas. This limitation highlights the need for an open-source, affordable, and customizable UAV platform tailored for high-throughput, low-latency 5G research.

Moreover, to the best of our knowledge, no comprehensive analyses exist on how 5G connectivity impacts UAV performance, particularly in terms of packet transmission latency and energy consumption. The 3rd Generation Partnership Project (3GPP) standard defines strict latency requirements for both Line-of-Sight (LoS) and Non-Line-of-Sight (NLoS) applications. For instance, in LoS scenarios, uplink Command and Control (C2) packet latency must be under 40ms, while video packet latency must remain below 1s. In NLoS conditions, the latency requirement for video packets is even more stringent, at under 140ms [[6](https://arxiv.org/html/2506.08386v1#bib.bib6)] [[7](https://arxiv.org/html/2506.08386v1#bib.bib7)]. Meeting these requirements is not just crucial for current UAV operations but also paves the way for Beyond-Line-of-Sight (BLoS) flights, where UAVs must operate without direct visual contact with the pilot. Without ensuring reliable 5G connectivity that meets LoS and NLoS latency constraints, achieving safe and effective BLoS operations will remain unattainable.

Contributions: In this paper, we make several key contributions. Firstly, we present a detailed workflow for updating an outdated commercial drone (Intel Aero) into a fully functional 5G-enabled UAV, creating a cost-effective, small-form-factor research platform for studying aerial 5G networks.
Secondly, we introduce the 5G Aero, a prototyping platform designed to evaluate whether existing 5G technologies can support UAV communications while meeting 3GPP latency and throughput benchmarks in both LoS and NLoS conditions.
Finally, we assess the 5G Aero’s performance under diverse scenarios and configurations to determine whether current 5G and UAV technologies available to the research community (e.g., 5G Radio Access Network (RAN) and core network software, flight controllers and 5G modems) comply with 3GPP-specified benchmarks for latency and throughput in real-world LoS and NLoS conditions. Additionally, we evaluate the impact of 5G connectivity on the UAV flight duration.

The remainder of the paper is organized as follows: Section [II](https://arxiv.org/html/2506.08386v1#S2 "II State of the Art ‣ 5G Aero: A Prototyping Platform for Evaluating Aerial 5G Communications") reviews the current state of the art in UAV for 5G applications, highlighting the limitations and potential of existing platforms. Section [III](https://arxiv.org/html/2506.08386v1#S3 "III 5G Aero Design and Prototype ‣ 5G Aero: A Prototyping Platform for Evaluating Aerial 5G Communications") describes the methodology and technical modifications required to upgrade a discontinued drone into the 5G Aero, including hardware enhancements and 5G integration. Section [IV](https://arxiv.org/html/2506.08386v1#S4 "IV Experiment Setup ‣ 5G Aero: A Prototyping Platform for Evaluating Aerial 5G Communications") details the experimental setup, outlining the testing environment, evaluation metrics, and procedures for assessing the UAV’s performance under LoS and NLoS conditions. Section [V](https://arxiv.org/html/2506.08386v1#S5 "V Results ‣ 5G Aero: A Prototyping Platform for Evaluating Aerial 5G Communications") presents the results, analyzing the UAV ’s battery life, latency and reliability against 3GPP benchmarks. Section [VI](https://arxiv.org/html/2506.08386v1#S6 "VI Results Overview and Challenges ‣ 5G Aero: A Prototyping Platform for Evaluating Aerial 5G Communications") discusses the challenges encountered during development and testing. Finally, section [VII](https://arxiv.org/html/2506.08386v1#S7 "VII Conclusions ‣ 5G Aero: A Prototyping Platform for Evaluating Aerial 5G Communications") concludes the paper with a summary of our key findings.

## II State of the Art

The integration of UAVs with 5G technologies is an emerging field that demands careful consideration of UAV capabilities, size, payload, and cost.
Monarch is a quadcopter equipped with a companion computer and a Universal Software Radio Peripheral (USRP) that offers a programmable UAV platform to operate in the sub-6GHz frequency range [[8](https://arxiv.org/html/2506.08386v1#bib.bib8)]. Thanks to the use of Software-defined Radio (SDR), Monarch enables complex wireless research with diverse wireless technologies and standards. However, its large size limits its application to outdoor scenarios and prevents its use for indoor applications.
In contrast, the AIRLink [[9](https://arxiv.org/html/2506.08386v1#bib.bib9)] is a compact Flight Control Unit (FCU) featuring a 5G connectivity module to support low latency and high data bandwidth. It supports up to 600 Mbps bandwidth and operates on 5G sub-6 and mmWave frequencies. However, despite the many features available in this small form-factor product, the AIRLink module only offers the FCU and networking capabilities, which needs to be integrated with a UAV, making this solution relatively expensive.
In [[10](https://arxiv.org/html/2506.08386v1#bib.bib10)], authors used the DJI Matrice 600, a hexacopter equipped with a USRP, to act as an aerial Next Generation Node Base (gNB). The UAV offers significant payload capacity, making it suitable for carrying heavy equipment. However, its high cost and large size make the platform expensive and unsuitable for flying in indoor environments.
A small and affordable UAV alternative is the Crazyflie 2.1. The Crazyflie has been considered in [[11](https://arxiv.org/html/2506.08386v1#bib.bib11)] and is a quadrotor of about 12 cm by 12 cm (including the propellers). It has been used to fly in small tunnels but it is not capable of holding a 5G radio. This limitation restricts its potential for 5G-based communication research, highlighting the trade-off between size, cost, and technological capability.
Finally, the Sky5G UAV [[12](https://arxiv.org/html/2506.08386v1#bib.bib12)] integrates 5G capabilities through a USRP mini and a form factor characterized by a 1-meter diagonal. Sky5G acts as an aerial base station, but does not offer User Equipment (UE) capabilities.
In this paper, we fill the gap in the literature by presenting 5G Aero, a compact, cost-effective 5G-enabled custom UE drone designed specifically for indoor applications. Unlike typical 5G drones that are often bulky and expensive, our design ensures a compact size for flying in confined spaces without compromising performance. Moreover, unlike previous works, we evaluate the 5G UAV performance by assessing whether it can meet the stringent requirements defined by 3GPP.

## III 5G Aero Design and Prototype

To build a compact and 5G capable drone, it is important to understand its architecture and the core components that dictate its functionality and performance. A UAV consists of several key elements. The frame provides structural support and houses all components securely. The computing unit handles data processing, communication, and control tasks. The FCU stabilizes the drone, processes sensor inputs, and executes flight commands. The radio front-ends enable wireless communication. Additionally, the UAV is battery powered, it has propellers driven by Electronic Speed Controllers (ESCs) and it can incorporate sensors (e.g. LiDAR, and optical flow) for navigation and environmental awareness.

In the following, we first describe the baseline UAV platform we used in this work (i.e., the Intel Aero), and then provide a detailed description of how we updated the original design to integrate 5G capabilities. Specifically, our design has been engineered to meet the following core principles: (i) support for 5G UE operations so that the UAV can act as an aerial mobile terminal; (ii) programmability and support for future extensions; (iii) small form-factor; (iv) contained cost.

![Refer to caption](x1.png)
![Refer to caption](x2.png)

Baseline Platform
With these design considerations in mind, we selected the Intel Aero as the baseline platform.
The Intel Aero is a discontinued UAV platform designed to provide a comprehensive development kit for UAV technology enthusiasts and professionals. Central to its architecture, there is a computing unit running Ubuntu 16, a dedicated FCU, and a versatile frame equipped with essential UAV components such as a GPS. The platform became popular for supporting a range of sensors and accessories aimed to enhance UAV capabilities.
Despite its obsolete operating system, outdated drivers, and lack of technical support and 5G compatibility, many of its components—such as the frame and motors—still meet the required size and suitability for indoor applications. For this reason, we decided to remove the obsolete elements from the Intel Aero and re-purpose its frame and rotors.
Fig. [1(a)](https://arxiv.org/html/2506.08386v1#S3.F1.sf1 "In Figure 1 ‣ III 5G Aero Design and Prototype ‣ 5G Aero: A Prototyping Platform for Evaluating Aerial 5G Communications") shows how the original Intel Aero was designed to minimize component count. For this reason, it featured an integrated main computing board hosting both the FCU and the Computing Unit. This integration indeed helps reduce the form factor but limits the ability to upgrade the design and add or remove components. The main computing board is powered by a power distribution board which, draws energy from a battery placed in a dedicated slot of the frame. The power distribution board also supplies power to the motors through ESCs, which connect to the FCU via a dedicated interface on the main computing board. Additional interfaces on the FCU allows the connections of external sensors such as GPS, lidar, and optical flow, as well as the UAV ’s radio controller receiver. The only interface offered by the Computing Unit is a micro USB B port, necessitating a converter to ensure compatibility with other connectors. This port must be split with additional extenders to connect more than one device to the Computing Unit. Finally, the FCU communicates with all the components through MAVLink messaging protocol, while the Computing Unit, which runs an outdated Linux version, directly interacts with the FCU through MAVLink Router, facilitating direct interactions with UAV telemetry.

5G Aero Design
Figure [1(b)](https://arxiv.org/html/2506.08386v1#S3.F1.sf2 "In Figure 1 ‣ III 5G Aero Design and Prototype ‣ 5G Aero: A Prototyping Platform for Evaluating Aerial 5G Communications") illustrates the design of our 5G Aero. The primary modification is the removal of the main computing board, which facilitates the disaggregation of the FCU and the Computing Unit. This separation enables the individual updating of components, allowing for the selection of a computing unit with higher computational power, a GPU, or specific ports. Another significant enhancement is the addition of power connectivity, which supports powering an additional sensor or radio directly from the power distribution board. These two redesigns significantly enhance the platform’s versatility. First, the computational limitations are now resolved as the computing unit can be personalized to specific use cases, whether requiring a dedicated GPU, additional memory, or I/O ports. Second, the added power connector allows for the integration of more advanced devices, such as a 5G radio, expanding the UAV ’s operational capabilities.

Fig. [2](https://arxiv.org/html/2506.08386v1#S3.F2 "Figure 2 ‣ III 5G Aero Design and Prototype ‣ 5G Aero: A Prototyping Platform for Evaluating Aerial 5G Communications") shows the 5G Aero UAV we have built following the design described above and shown in Fig. [1(b)](https://arxiv.org/html/2506.08386v1#S3.F1.sf2 "In Figure 1 ‣ III 5G Aero Design and Prototype ‣ 5G Aero: A Prototyping Platform for Evaluating Aerial 5G Communications"). As FCU we have installed the latest and smallest model of the Pixhawk family, the 6C mini. We have paired it with the GMKtec NucBox G2, a small factor Computing Unit, 3.3x3.3x1.37 inches (8.5x8.5x3.5 cm), with 12GB of RAM, and an Intel 12th Alder Lake N100 processor, which perfectly fits within the UAV’s frame. The UAV ’s original ESCs, which had proprietary protocols incompatible with MAVLink’s open protocol DShot, were replaced to enable telemetry data transmission, essential for the FCU to optimize stability and altitude control. We have connected a commercial 5G modem (Sierra Wireless EM9191 NR 5G Modem) to the Computing Unit and the power distribution board using a suitable power regulator. Additionally, we have connected an altitude lidar to the FCU for continuous altitude control during flights.

MAVLink Router has been installed on the Computing Board to facilitate efficient message management between the FCU, drone sensors and ground station. Finally, we implemented the full protocol stack from OpenAirInterface [[13](https://arxiv.org/html/2506.08386v1#bib.bib13)] to establish and maintain a secure 5G connection between the UAV and the gNB (Fig. [3(b)](https://arxiv.org/html/2506.08386v1#S4.F3.sf2 "In Figure 3 ‣ IV Experiment Setup ‣ 5G Aero: A Prototyping Platform for Evaluating Aerial 5G Communications")). Since we intend to operate this drone exclusively indoors, where GPS is ineffective and adds unnecessary weight, no GPS was installed.

![Refer to caption](x3.png)

## IV Experiment Setup

We aim to evaluate 5G Aero’s performance in diverse experimental scenarios and evaluate its compliance with the 3GPP-specified benchmarks for UAV operations and related to latency and reliability. This work specifically focuses on an indoor scenario: an office environment. This setup, described below, contains numerous obstacles, clutter, and reflective objects that can attenuate or disrupt the signal between the UAV and the gNB.

![Refer to caption](x4.png)
![Refer to caption](x5.png)

### IV-A Experiment Overview

To determine if a small form-factor UAVs equipped with 5G technology can fulfill the stringent requirements of UAV-based applications (which will be detailed below), we conducted a flight test in an indoor environment designed to simulate a UAV flying inside an office or laboratory. This environment enabled UAV to navigate through LoS and NLoS spaces, with obstacles made of various materials that influence signal propagation due to multi-path and attenuation. The objective of this experiment is to assess whether the 5G UAV could satisfy the 3GPP flight requirements stipulated in the latest ETSI release [[6](https://arxiv.org/html/2506.08386v1#bib.bib6), [7](https://arxiv.org/html/2506.08386v1#bib.bib7)]. At least 99.9% of C2 packets, both in the uplink and downlink, must have a latency below 40ms. Also, at least 99.9% of uplink video packets in LoS must have a latency of no more than 1s, while for video in NLoS, at least 99.99% of packets must have a latency under 140ms.

The office space used in our experiments is illustrated in Fig. [3(a)](https://arxiv.org/html/2506.08386v1#S4.F3.sf1 "In Figure 3 ‣ IV Experiment Setup ‣ 5G Aero: A Prototyping Platform for Evaluating Aerial 5G Communications") and comprises a 5G Aero UAV and a gNB. The latter is implemented via a USRP X410 connected to a host machine (left part of Fig. [3(b)](https://arxiv.org/html/2506.08386v1#S4.F3.sf2 "In Figure 3 ‣ IV Experiment Setup ‣ 5G Aero: A Prototyping Platform for Evaluating Aerial 5G Communications")) where the 5G protocol stack (both RAN and core network) runs on the OpenAirInterface (OAI) open-source software. First, the 5G Aero establishes connectivity with the gNB via the Sierra Wireless 5G module, then generates both C2 and video streaming traffic. Our goal is to measure and evaluate latency and throughput for all packets.

The system operates at a 3.6 GHz central frequency and a 40 MHz bandwidth. On the gNB side, the C2 packets are captured by the ground control station application MissionPlanner, while the video is captured by a VLC application running in the background.
In our experiments, we consider four distinct scenarios as described in Fig. [3(a)](https://arxiv.org/html/2506.08386v1#S4.F3.sf1 "In Figure 3 ‣ IV Experiment Setup ‣ 5G Aero: A Prototyping Platform for Evaluating Aerial 5G Communications"). For each scenario, the experiment is executed three times for two minutes.

The following scenarios are analyzed: In the first two, static flight and LoS conditions are considered, with the 5G Aero hovering 3m and 7m away from the gNB, respectively. In the third scenario, we consider static and NLoS conditions with the 5G Aero 14m away from the gNB. Finally, the forth case is a dynamic flight case where the 5G Aero is initially located 3m away from the gNB (LoS) and then reaches the last position, i.e., 14m away from the gNB in NLoS conditions.

During each phase, the UAV transmits both flight and UAV telemetry data, as well as video streams at different resolutions: 480p, 720p, and 1080p. To comply with regulations, the drone is manually controlled by a pilot who handles takeoff, the UAV maintains an altitude of 1.5m via lidar-based altitude control until it lands. In the dynamic scenario, the pilot also controls the UAV’s movements.

![Refer to caption](x6.png)
![Refer to caption](x7.png)
![Refer to caption](x8.png)
![Refer to caption](x9.png)

## V Results

To evaluate the performance experienced by the 5G Aero and obtain end-user metrics such as latency and throughput, data collection is performed by capturing packets at both UE and Base Station (BS) sides using Wireshark.

### V-A Uplink Traffic Analysis

Fig. [4(a)](https://arxiv.org/html/2506.08386v1#S4.F4.sf1 "In Figure 4 ‣ IV-A Experiment Overview ‣ IV Experiment Setup ‣ 5G Aero: A Prototyping Platform for Evaluating Aerial 5G Communications") shows that the average packet size in the uplink for both video and C2 remains consistent at different flight distances, as well as the interval rate at which video packets are transmitted as shown in Fig. [4(b)](https://arxiv.org/html/2506.08386v1#S4.F4.sf2 "In Figure 4 ‣ IV-A Experiment Overview ‣ IV Experiment Setup ‣ 5G Aero: A Prototyping Platform for Evaluating Aerial 5G Communications"). Conversely, the interval rate for the C2 packets varies depending on the flight dynamics.

![Refer to caption](x10.png)
![Refer to caption](x11.png)
![Refer to caption](x12.png)
![Refer to caption](x13.png)

From a different perspective, Fig. 4c illustrates the average packet interval at various video qualities. As previously explained, the frequency rate of C2 packets fluctuates, while the frequency of video packets changes due to the higher throughput required by the different video qualities.
The increase in throughput may create a bottleneck in the uplink, hence increasing drastically the latency or the packet error rate.
Specifically, Figs. [5(a)](https://arxiv.org/html/2506.08386v1#S5.F5.sf1 "In Figure 5 ‣ V-A Uplink Traffic Analysis ‣ V Results ‣ 5G Aero: A Prototyping Platform for Evaluating Aerial 5G Communications") and [5(b)](https://arxiv.org/html/2506.08386v1#S5.F5.sf2 "In Figure 5 ‣ V-A Uplink Traffic Analysis ‣ V Results ‣ 5G Aero: A Prototyping Platform for Evaluating Aerial 5G Communications") illustrate the uplink latency for C2 and video packets across different video transmission qualities of 480p, 720p, and 1080p and operational scenarios. In the figure, we also use the dashed horizontal lines to represent the 3GPP-recommended maximum reference values for latency.

![Refer to caption](x14.png)
![Refer to caption](x15.png)

#### V-A1 480p

Fig. [5(a)](https://arxiv.org/html/2506.08386v1#S5.F5.sf1 "In Figure 5 ‣ V-A Uplink Traffic Analysis ‣ V Results ‣ 5G Aero: A Prototyping Platform for Evaluating Aerial 5G Communications") (top) shows that the latency, experienced by the 5G Aero when the resolution is 480p, remains below the desired 40ms threshold for almost the entirety of the experiment. However, we notice that an exception occurs during the takeoff phase when the distance is set to 3m. In this case, we expect a strong reflected path from the ground, which can cause multi-path interference, momentarily increasing latency and resulting in 99.31% of packets having a latency below the requested threshold. A similar latency spike is observed at takeoff for video packets, as shown in Fig. [5(b)](https://arxiv.org/html/2506.08386v1#S5.F5.sf2 "In Figure 5 ‣ V-A Uplink Traffic Analysis ‣ V Results ‣ 5G Aero: A Prototyping Platform for Evaluating Aerial 5G Communications") (top). Specifically, we notice that latency reaches up to 830ms, yet this value still falls within the acceptable latency threshold of 140ms defined by 3GPP. Besides the take-off in the 3m experiment, transmission latencies always remain within the specified latency requirements throughout all the LoS and NLoS experiments.

#### V-A2 720p

As we increase the resolution to 720p, the transmission frequency for video packets doubles to 0.006 packets/s, compared to 0.013 packets/s at 480p, potentially increasing congestion on the 5G link. Fig. [5(a)](https://arxiv.org/html/2506.08386v1#S5.F5.sf1 "In Figure 5 ‣ V-A Uplink Traffic Analysis ‣ V Results ‣ 5G Aero: A Prototyping Platform for Evaluating Aerial 5G Communications") (center) shows the latency uplink for C2 packets, revealing notable latency spikes during the 14m and dynamic experiments, both conducted in NLoS conditions. Specifically, we notice that the 5G Aero in the 14m static experiment achieves a reliability of 99.9%, with only one packet exceeding a 40ms latency. Conversely, the Dynamic experiment, which alternates between LoS and NLoS, exhibits a latency reliability of 99.5%.
To better understand why latency values increase substantially in NLoS and dynamic scenarios, Fig. [6](https://arxiv.org/html/2506.08386v1#S5.F6 "Figure 6 ‣ V-A Uplink Traffic Analysis ‣ V Results ‣ 5G Aero: A Prototyping Platform for Evaluating Aerial 5G Communications") (center) shows the Reference Signal Received Power (RSRP) levels over time between the UAV and the gNB. Throughout the 14m experiment, it is evident that the RSRP levels are generally low, with an average value of approximately -100 dBm, largely due to obstacles and absorbing materials. Similarly, in the dynamic experiment, the signal strength fluctuates depending on the drone’s position relative to LoS or NLoS. When in LoS, the signal maintains acceptable levels (approximately -85 dBm); however, as it transitions to a NLoS scenario, the signal strength diminishes to as low as -100 dBm, resulting in increased delays in packet delivery. Despite this, video packet latencies in Fig. [5(b)](https://arxiv.org/html/2506.08386v1#S5.F5.sf2 "In Figure 5 ‣ V-A Uplink Traffic Analysis ‣ V Results ‣ 5G Aero: A Prototyping Platform for Evaluating Aerial 5G Communications") remain under 1s, with the worst-case scenario at 0.093s. Finally, transmission latencies for C2 packets from the 3m and 7m LoS experiments are always under the latency requirements.

#### V-A3 1080p

Increasing the video resolution to 1080p results in a transmission rate of 0.003 packets/s, which is double that of 720p and four times that of 480p, further saturating the uplink channel. This increase, combined with low signal quality in NLoS conditions, which degrades to -103 dBm as shown in Fig. [6](https://arxiv.org/html/2506.08386v1#S5.F6 "Figure 6 ‣ V-A Uplink Traffic Analysis ‣ V Results ‣ 5G Aero: A Prototyping Platform for Evaluating Aerial 5G Communications") (right), induces latency spikes. From Fig. [5(a)](https://arxiv.org/html/2506.08386v1#S5.F5.sf1 "In Figure 5 ‣ V-A Uplink Traffic Analysis ‣ V Results ‣ 5G Aero: A Prototyping Platform for Evaluating Aerial 5G Communications") (bottom), the 14m experiment in NLoS conditions, displays a reliability of 98.73%, and the dynamic flight experiences spikes solely in NLoS conditions, achieving a reliability of 99.58%. From Fig. [5(b)](https://arxiv.org/html/2506.08386v1#S5.F5.sf2 "In Figure 5 ‣ V-A Uplink Traffic Analysis ‣ V Results ‣ 5G Aero: A Prototyping Platform for Evaluating Aerial 5G Communications"), video packets consistently meet the sub-1s latency requirement in both LoS and NLoS.

### V-B Downlink Traffic Analysis

The only data transmitted in downlink are TCP acknowledgment packets related to telemetry traffic. The average packet size is 66.8 bytes, and the 3GPP latency requirements for this type of downlink traffic are set to 40ms. From Fig. [5(c)](https://arxiv.org/html/2506.08386v1#S5.F5.sf3 "In Figure 5 ‣ V-A Uplink Traffic Analysis ‣ V Results ‣ 5G Aero: A Prototyping Platform for Evaluating Aerial 5G Communications"), it is evident that all acknowledgment packets, across all video qualities, are delivered within the required latency, with the highest latency recorded at 28ms.

Battery Consumption:
In our tests, the 5G Aero battery, with a capacity of 83Wh, provides an average of 5 minutes and 2 seconds of flight time without the 5G module turned on. When 5G transmissions are active, the average flight duration decreases by approximately 1%, resulting in an average flight time of roughly 4 minutes and 59 seconds. This reduction demonstrates that the 5G connectivity module in the Sierra Wireless board increases battery drain. However, it represents a relatively negligible decrease if compared to the consumption caused by propellers and flight operations.

## VI Results Overview and Challenges

Our experiments have shown that the 5G Aero is able to meet the latency requirements set by 3GPP, with a few exceptions during NLoS conditions where high throughput demands from videos and obstacles cause interference. It is also noteworthy that the frequency of C2 packet transmission in both uplink and downlink ranges from 0.026 packets/s to 0.032 packets/s, which exceeds the expectations set by the 3GPP standards of 0.04 packets/s.

### VI-A Challenges:

During the development, we faced several challenges.

i) Size and Configuration:
The main challenge was designing the drone to fit all components within the compact Intel Aero frame. This required careful placement, disaggregation of the FCU and Computing Unit, and the addition of a power converter, while optimizing space and preventing short circuits. Despite the addition of the 5G module, battery consumption concerns remained minimal, with only a negligible reduction in flight time. Additionally, an alternative approach to furth

enriched_points.expanded_content: 5G technology revolutionizes UAV operations by providing ultra-reliable, low-latency communications, enabling real-time control and data exchange over vast distances.
With 5G, UAVs transmit high-definition video and sensor data instantaneously, facilitating immediate decision-making in critical applications like emergency response and industrial inspections.
Drones equipped with 5G can conduct detailed inspections of infrastructure like bridges and power lines, providing real-time feedback and reducing the need for manual assessments.
5G-enabled UAVs enhance traffic monitoring by streaming live feeds and data analytics, allowing for improved management of urban transport systems.
The integration of 5G significantly enhances UAV operational capabilities, allowing for beyond-visual-line-of-sight missions and enabling swarm operations for complex tasks.
